briefing.headline: Crank it — pre-spawn clear water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: Fish the Red Eye Shad like a worm or jig, not just cast-and-reel. Rip it out of cover, let it flutter down — that swimming shimmy on the fall is the magic. The colder it is, the slower you fish. Downsize to 1/4oz on ultra-shallow flats (1-2ft). Upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the fall and

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: KVD: Fish the Red Eye Shad like a worm or jig, not just cast-and-reel. Rip it out of cover, let it flutter down — that swimming shimmy on the fall is the magic. The colder it is, the slower you fish.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.

KVD: 'I pick up my fishing pace considerably as the water hits around 52°F. I may run down a bank quickly throwing a lipless crankbait or spinnerbait, then pause to pitch a jig at submerged logs or brushpiles.'

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal pre-spawn bass are aggressive and moving shallow. Expect fish in the 4-8ft range on secondary points and channel swings. Falling pressure pulls fish up from staging areas — target the first breakline off spawning flats.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — pre-spawn clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — pre-spawn clear water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Cover water — pre-spawn clear conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — pre-spawn clear conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — pre-spawn clear water is prime soft plastic territory.

In pre-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable pre-spawn conditions mean fish are staging predictably at 5-12ft on points, channel swings, and riprap banks. They're feeding aggressively as water warms. Work the transition zone between deep winter haunts and shallow spawning areas.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: During spring the prime depth zone on most lakes is 6 feet or less; the Jabber Jaw Hybrid Squarebill dives 3-5 feet which is the right zone for most fisheries across the country.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: For a spring pre-spawn event limited to three rods, picks a jerkbait/crankbait for deeper staging fish, a 1/2-oz jig for versatility, and a ChatterBait for stained shallow water.

KVD: 'I pick up my fishing pace considerably as the water hits around 52°F. I may run down a bank quickly throwing a lipless crankbait or spinnerbait, then pause to pitch a jig at submerged logs or brushpiles.'

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable pre-spawn conditions mean fish are staging predictably at 5-12ft on points, channel swings, and riprap banks. They're feeding aggressively as water warms. Work the transition zone between deep winter haunts and shallow spawning areas.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — pre-spawn stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with stained water and stable conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal pre-spawn bass are aggressive and moving shallow. Expect fish in the 4-8ft range on secondary points and channel swings. Falling pressure pulls fish up from staging areas — target the first breakline off spawning flats.

briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, creek channel, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Texas rig a Ned bait on a swing head jig to fish snaggy areas where an exposed hook empties your terminal box.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, creek channel, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: 'Rising water — bass push shallow and spread out. Best spring days can occur when warming water rises fast. Key on runoff areas: backs of creeks, culverts, residential yards, city street drainage. Water temp can vary 5 degrees between pockets...

Palaniuk: 'The spot had a riprap bank with a steep drop, then a trough that came up onto a hard bottom ridge, tapered back off into another trough. The trough made a horseshoe. A 40-yard section where the fish wanted to live, with a 25-foot hole t...

Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal pre-spawn bass are aggressive and moving shallow. Expect fish in the 4-8ft range on secondary points and channel swings. Falling pressure pulls fish up from staging areas — target the first breakline off spawning flats.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If you get followers without commits, add scent or downsize your hook/weight

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — pre-spawn muddy water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Cover water — pre-spawn muddy conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Card (pro): 'Single Tail Grub works all year but great during pre-spawn for smallmouth. Fish it like a small swimbait, reeling slow enough to just tick rocks. Also reel-reel-reel then let fall on tight line'

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal pre-spawn bass are aggressive and moving shallow. Expect fish in the 4-8ft range on secondary points and channel swings. Falling pressure pulls fish up from staging areas — target the first breakline off spawning flats.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If moving baits aren't getting bit, slow down to a crawling retrieve or switch to a jig

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — pre-spawn muddy conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — pre-spawn muddy water is prime soft plastic territory.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Crank it — pre-spawn muddy water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: Fish the Red Eye Shad like a worm or jig, not just cast-and-reel. Rip it out of cover, let it flutter down — that swimming shimmy on the fall is the magic. The colder it is, the slower you fish. Downsize to 1/4oz on ultra-shallow flats (1-2ft). Upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the fall and

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable pre-spawn conditions mean fish are staging predictably at 5-12ft on points, channel swings, and riprap banks. They're feeding aggressively as water warms. Work the transition zone between deep winter haunts and shallow spawning areas.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

In pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better act
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: Thunder Cricket in shallow grass even in upper 30s water. Use 3/8 oz (lighter than normal) with straight-tail blade minnow trailer for subtle action. Pull and stop, not steady retrieve. Get it do
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: In cold/post-frontal pre-spawn, don't just wind the Thunder Cricket. Work it like a jig — hop, twitch, pull-and-stop. Get it down in the grass and on the bottom. Bites are extremely subtle: the bait just gets heavy or the vibration stops. 'I ...

Card (pro): 'Single Tail Grub works all year but great during pre-spawn for smallmouth. Fish it like a small swimbait, reeling slow enough to just tick rocks. Also reel-reel-reel then let fall on tight line'

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal pre-spawn fish pull back to 6-12ft staging areas. They won't commit shallow after the front. Target the first breakline off spawning flats and key on deeper structure near shallow spawning zones.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If moving baits aren't getting bit, slow down to a crawling retrieve or switch to a jig

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — pre-spawn muddy conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Match the conditions — slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Wheeler: On flat fisheries bass stay in the shallow zone a long time before spawning, but on highland reservoirs they may stage in 6-10 feet before moving up; crankbait selection should match the lake type and depth zone.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — pre-spawn muddy water is prime soft plastic territory.
briefing.gameplan:
In pre-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Crank it — spawn clear water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: 'Don't assume you must finesse spawning bass. At the 2011 Classic, 4 of top 7 finishers power fished spinnerbaits and crankbaits on the same 150-yard spawning flat. Key conditions: hard bottom flat with stumps + grass, 10-degree water temp ri...
Johnston: Uses Garmin LiveScope Perspective Mode to spot individual bed fish in rippled conditions when you can't see them visually. Cast to them and catch on first attempt.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal spawning bass are active on beds in 2-5ft. Falling pressure often triggers new waves of fish to move onto beds. Target hard-bottom spawning flats, secondary pockets, and protected coves.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — spawn clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, dock, grass. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Yamamoto pro Fukae: Uses 14-lb YGK sinking braid to an 8-lb fluorocarbon leader for spawn-time drop-shot and Ned Rig presentations on a Shimano Expride 170M with 2500-size spinning reel

Johnston: Use Garmin Perspective Mode when seeing bed fish in low-visibility conditions. This was one of two main tools (along with flipping and drop shot baits) for bed fishing at the St. Johns River.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — spawn clear water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — spawn clear water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, topwater presentations shine.

In spawn with clear water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: Match the conditions — moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, dock. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: Neko-rigged creature baits are 'deadly springtime bait around the spawn.' In clear water, most fish bite on the initial fall — if they're on a bed, the bait lands next to them and they eat it immediately. Works for both largemouth and smallmo...

Yamamoto pro Fukae: The Free Rig is a cross between a Texas Rig and Carolina Rig — the line runs freely through the weight's eye so the weight falls first and the soft plastic flutters down behind it with an unpredictable slow fall; use a 'lift an...

Hackney: 'In the early spawn, nothing beats a piece of plastic. Use baits without a lot of action like a tube or Rodent - the only action is from your rod tip. Be patient with repetitive casts to bedding bass.'
Johnston: Uses Garmin LiveScope Perspective Mode to spot individual bed fish in rippled conditions when you can't see them visually. Cast to them and catch on first attempt.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable spawn conditions have fish committed to beds in 1-5ft. Focus on hard-bottom areas in protected coves, pockets, and flats. Polarized glasses are essential for sight-fishing.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If bites dry up, try shaking in place for 10-15 seconds before moving — patience pays with plastics

briefing.headline: Crank it — spawn clear water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal spawning bass become extremely lockjaw but won't abandon beds. Fish are still in 2-5ft but much harder to trigger. Slow way down and make repeated presentations to visible beds.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — spawn clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — spawn clear water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — spawn stained water sets up explosive topwater action.

In spawn with stained water and stable conditions, topwater presentations shine.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — spawn stained water is prime soft plastic territory.

briefing.headline: Crank it — spawn stained water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal spawning bass become extremely lockjaw but won't abandon beds. Fish are still in 2-5ft but much harder to trigger. Slow way down and make repeated presentations to visible beds.

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — spawn stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal spawning bass are active on beds in 2-5ft. Falling pressure often triggers new waves of fish to move onto beds. Target hard-bottom spawning flats, secondary pockets, and protected coves.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If bites dry up, try shaking in place for 10-15 seconds before moving — patience pays with plastics

briefing.headline: Crank it — spawn muddy water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In spawn with muddy water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If bites are subtle, try a lighter jig weight — slower fall rate can be the difference

briefing.headline: Cover water — post-spawn clear conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: On lighter jigheads (1/8-3/16 oz) for Mooch Minnow swimbaits, cut off the tail tabs so the bait rolls more on the lighter head — gives more action for fish higher in the water column. On heavier heads (3/8-1/2 oz) keep the tail tabs intact as they won't affect action with the heavier weight

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — post-spawn clear water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Use monofilament (Sufix Siege) for topwater fishing — the stretch prevents jerking the bait away from the fish on the hookset. Pull into the fish rather than jerking, letting the sharp VMC hooks get into the fish

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — post-spawn clear conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — post-spawn clear water is prime soft plastic territory.

In post-spawn with clear water and pre frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Crank it — post-spawn clear water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable post-spawn conditions split fish between shallow fry-guarders (2-5ft) and transitioning females (5-15ft). Shad spawn activity on seawalls, riprap, and bluff walls concentrates bigger fish shallow at dawn.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — post-spawn clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and stable conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — post-spawn clear water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and stable conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal post-spawn fish hunker down at 6-12ft near cover. Males may temporarily leave fry. Focus on the deeper side of transition areas — channel edges, deeper points, and offshore structure near spawning flats.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If bites are subtle, try a lighter jig weight — slower fall rate can be the difference

briefing.headline: Cover water — post-spawn clear conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: On lighter jigheads (1/8-3/16 oz) for Mooch Minnow swimbaits, cut off the tail tabs so the bait rolls more on the lighter head — gives more action for fish higher in the water column. On heavier heads (3/8-1/2 oz) keep the tail tabs intact as they won't affect action with the heavier weight

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — post-spawn clear conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — post-spawn clear water is prime soft plastic territory.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with clear water and post frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Crank it — post-spawn stained water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal post-spawn bass are transitioning and aggressive. Males guard fry in 2-6ft while females roam 5-12ft feeding heavily. Target both shallow fry-guarders and roaming post-spawn females near points.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — post-spawn stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — post-spawn stained water sets up explosive topwater action.

In post-spawn with stained water and pre frontal conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Cover water — post-spawn stained conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with stained water and stable conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: On lighter jigheads (1/8-3/16 oz) for Mooch Minnow swimbaits, cut off the tail tabs so the bait rolls more on the lighter head — gives more action for fish higher in the water column. On heavier heads (3/8-1/2 oz) keep the tail tabs intact as they won't affect action with the heavier weight

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — post-spawn stained water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with stained water and stable conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Use monofilament (Sufix Siege) for topwater fishing — the stretch prevents jerking the bait away from the fish on the hookset. Pull into the fish rather than jerking, letting the sharp VMC hooks get into the fish

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — post-spawn stained conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.

In post-spawn with stained water and stable conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — post-spawn stained water is prime soft plastic territory.

briefing.headline: Crank it — post-spawn stained water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. Deflection and disturbance trigger strikes.'

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — post-spawn stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with stained water and post frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Cover water — post-spawn muddy conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: On lighter jigheads (1/8-3/16 oz) for Mooch Minnow swimbaits, cut off the tail tabs so the bait rolls more on the lighter head — gives more action for fish higher in the water column. On heavier heads (3/8-1/2 oz) keep the tail tabs intact as they won't affect action with the heavier weight

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — post-spawn muddy conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Crank it — post-spawn muddy water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

Robertson: When the swimbait bite slows and fish scatter, pick up a deep-diving crankbait like the SPRO Little John DD 70 to put the majority of your weight in the boat. It produced the bulk of a 32-pound bag.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable post-spawn conditions split fish between shallow fry-guarders (2-5ft) and transitioning females (5-15ft). Shad spawn activity on seawalls, riprap, and bluff walls concentrates bigger fish shallow at dawn.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — post-spawn muddy conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with muddy water and stable conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — post-spawn muddy conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In post-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

In post-spawn with muddy water and post frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Crank it — summer clear water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

Robertson: When postspawners leave the tapering points, follow them with a Berkley Dredger 20.5 or 25.5 down ditches and drainages 10-15 feet deep that lead from spawning flats toward the main lake.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Robertson: When postspawners leave the tapering points, follow them with a Berkley Dredger 20.5 or 25.5 down ditches and drainages 10-15 feet deep that lead from spawning flats toward the main lake.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. De
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: Reading the mood of offshore bass is essential - sometimes a deep crankbait triggers reaction bites from bigger fish when finesse approaches are dominating.

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal summer bass feed aggressively. Fish split between shallow morning patterns (2-6ft) and offshore structure (12-25ft). Target grass lines, points, and ledges as falling pressure triggers movement.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — summer clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish an 80-yard stretch of bottom in about 40 feet targeting a pod of giant smallmouth. Catch one, then leave the spot and make a big run away before returning, because pressured deepwater smallmouth shut down after each catch.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — summer clear water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Cover water — summer clear conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, ledge, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — summer clear water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with clear water and pre frontal conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Used an X-Rap Prop and handcarved prop bait in bluegill patterns around bluegill beds; when a bass dogs the topwater darting back and forth without striking, pause the bait for as long as 6 seconds until the fish inhales it.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — summer clear water is prime soft plastic territory.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Crank it — summer clear water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with clear water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

Robertson: When postspawners leave the tapering points, follow them with a Berkley Dredger 20.5 or 25.5 down ditches and drainages 10-15 feet deep that lead from spawning flats toward the main lake.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — summer stained water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with stained water and pre frontal conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Used an X-Rap Prop and handcarved prop bait in bluegill patterns around bluegill beds; when a bass dogs the topwater darting back and forth without striking, pause the bait for as long as 6 seconds until the fish inhales it.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — summer stained water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with stained water and stable conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Used an X-Rap Prop and handcarved prop bait in bluegill patterns around bluegill beds; when a bass dogs the topwater darting back and forth without striking, pause the bait for as long as 6 seconds until the fish inhales it.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, point, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Palaniuk: 'Walk the frog on top of thick grass to get bass to blow through it, or fish it on edges and points of the grass. Walk it 90% of the time like a Zara Spook'

briefing.headline: Crank it — summer stained water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with stained water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

Robertson: When postspawners leave the tapering points, follow them with a Berkley Dredger 20.5 or 25.5 down ditches and drainages 10-15 feet deep that lead from spawning flats toward the main lake.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Robertson: When postspawners leave the tapering points, follow them with a Berkley Dredger 20.5 or 25.5 down ditches and drainages 10-15 feet deep that lead from spawning flats toward the main lake.
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. De
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: Reading the mood of offshore bass is essential - sometimes a deep crankbait triggers reaction bites from bigger fish when finesse approaches are dominating.

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.

Palaniuk: 'After you burn a crankbait through and catch a few, the ones that dont want to bite kind of move out of the way and break up. A swimbait allows them to settle back in but still gives them a moving target to chase.'

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal summer fish retreat to reliable offshore structure at 12-25ft. They're less aggressive but still need to eat. Focus on primary structure — main lake points, ledges, and humps with shade or current.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — summer stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with stained water and post frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish an 80-yard stretch of bottom in about 40 feet targeting a pod of giant smallmouth. Catch one, then leave the spot and make a big run away before returning, because pressured deepwater smallmouth shut down after each catch.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Crank it — summer muddy water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In summer with muddy water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

Robertson: When postspawners leave the tapering points, follow them with a Berkley Dredger 20.5 or 25.5 down ditches and drainages 10-15 feet deep that lead from spawning flats toward the main lake.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: 'The bait HAS to hit something — rocks, grass, bottom. Simply retrieving in a straight line won't yield results. If the bait isn't hitting anything, stop reeling and let it sink until it does. De
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: Reading the mood of offshore bass is essential - sometimes a deep crankbait triggers reaction bites from bigger fish when finesse approaches are dominating.

Johnston: On the Potomac, I found fish out in the grass, not in the real thick stuff. I was casting to catch them, not flipping. When that failed due to muddy water from rain, I moved to flipping grass on the Greenway Flats.

KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.

Palaniuk: 'After you burn a crankbait through and catch a few, the ones that dont want to bite kind of move out of the way and break up. A swimbait allows them to settle back in but still gives them a moving target to chase.'

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal summer fish retreat to reliable offshore structure at 12-25ft. They're less aggressive but still need to eat. Focus on primary structure — main lake points, ledges, and humps with shade or current.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Crank it — fall clear water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is at its best in cold water. It always makes bottom contact and finds inside weed edges. Ripping it free triggers strikes. It has a shimmy as it sinks like a stick worm — dynamite for yo-yoing inside edges. Sweep rod to the side, let it sink, repeat, moving bait 4-6 feet each swe

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: Even in slick calm clear conditions, a jerkbait over the top of them will draw fish from distance. 'They're going to see it and they're going to come.' Don't switch to finesse just because it's c

proInsights.insight: KVD: 'Biggest smallmouth cranking difference: it's NOT about bottom contact. Keep crankbait a few feet off bottom — smallmouth in clear water love to feed looking up. It's about speed and covering water, casting all directions to find roaming aggr...
Wheeler: In fall, fish school up in ditches, on little flats in 2-4 feet, and especially around grass; on grassless lakes, key on shallow ditches with stumps that let bass ambush shad.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal fall bass are following shad into the backs of creeks. Fish can be anywhere from 3-18ft depending on bait position. Target creek arms, secondary points, and anywhere baitfish are concentrating.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — fall clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are blowing up but not connecting, pause 3-5 seconds after each strike before resuming

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — fall clear conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and pre frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

Wheeler: In fall, fish school up in ditches, on little flats in 2-4 feet, and especially around grass; on grassless lakes, key on shallow ditches with stumps that let bass ambush shad.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — fall clear water is prime soft plastic territory.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and pre frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Crank it — fall clear water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is at its best in cold water. It always makes bottom contact and finds inside weed edges. Ripping it free triggers strikes. It has a shimmy as it sinks like a stick worm — dynamite for yo-yoing inside edges. Sweep rod to the side, let it sink, repeat, moving bait 4-6 feet each swe

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: 'Biggest smallmouth cranking difference: it's NOT about bottom contact. Keep crankbait a few feet off bottom — smallmouth in clear water love to feed looking up. It's about speed and covering water, casting all directions to find roaming aggr...
Wheeler: In fall, fish school up in ditches, on little flats in 2-4 feet, and especially around grass; on grassless lakes, key on shallow ditches with stumps that let bass ambush shad.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable fall conditions create the most predictable fall fishing. Bass follow shad migrations into creek arms and pockets, feeding at 3-15ft. Key on points at creek mouths and secondary flats where bait concentrates.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — fall clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and stable conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

primaryApproach.targets: Focus on flat, grass, creek channel. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

Wheeler: In fall, fish school up in ditches, on little flats in 2-4 feet, and especially around grass; on grassless lakes, key on shallow ditches with stumps that let bass ambush shad.

Johnston: Fall smallmouth fishing is all about location and timing. Cover water fast with reaction baits to find active groups, then slow down with finesse presentations to catch the rest. If fish stop biting a tube, switch to Ned rig or drop shot.

If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors
If you get followers without commits, add scent or downsize your hook/weight

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — fall clear water puts a jig in its best element.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and stable conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Cover water — fall clear conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and stable conditions, moving baits presentations shine.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — fall clear water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with clear water and stable conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Uses a constant steady retrieve twitching with a little slack; when a fish blows up and misses, continue working the bait - slow it down but snap it harder to make it look injured but still running away from the fish.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

If water clarity decreases from wind or rain, upsize lures and switch to brighter colors

In fall with clear water and post frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.headline: Crank it — fall stained water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with stained water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — fall stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with stained water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Cover water — fall stained conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.

briefing.headline: Go over the top — fall stained water sets up explosive topwater action.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with stained water and pre frontal conditions, topwater presentations shine.

Wheeler: Uses a constant steady retrieve twitching with a little slack; when a fish blows up and misses, continue working the bait - slow it down but snap it harder to make it look injured but still running away from the fish.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — fall stained conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.

In fall with stained water and pre frontal conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable fall conditions create the most predictable fall fishing. Bass follow shad migrations into creek arms and pockets, feeding at 3-15ft. Key on points at creek mouths and secondary flats where bait concentrates.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If fish are blowing up but not connecting, pause 3-5 seconds after each strike before resuming

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — fall stained conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with stained water and stable conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Crank it — fall stained water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with stained water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is at its best in cold water. It always makes bottom contact and finds inside weed edges. Ripping it free triggers strikes. Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal fall fish pull tighter to cover and deeper structure at 8-18ft. The shad migration pauses but doesn't stop. Focus on the deepest fish in a given creek arm — channel bends and main creek points.

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — fall stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with stained water and post frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

In fall with stained water and post frontal conditions, jigs presentations shine.

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal fall bass are following shad into the backs of creeks. Fish can be anywhere from 3-18ft depending on bait position. Target creek arms, secondary points, and anywhere baitfish are concentrating.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — fall muddy water is prime soft plastic territory.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with muddy water and pre frontal conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Crank it — fall muddy water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with muddy water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: 'Red Eye Shad is at its best in cold water. It always makes bottom contact and finds inside weed edges. Ripping it free triggers strikes. Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable fall conditions create the most predictable fall fishing. Bass follow shad migrations into creek arms and pockets, feeding at 3-15ft. Key on points at creek mouths and secondary flats where bait concentrates.

If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — fall muddy conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with muddy water and stable conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — fall muddy water puts a jig in its best element.

In fall with muddy water and stable conditions, jigs presentations shine.

Johnston: Use a compact mini flipping jig instead of full-size. You catch more fish and get more bites. With ever-increasing fishing pressure, a smaller jig is what it takes to get bit.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — fall muddy conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In fall with muddy water and stable conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Wheeler: Aggressively snaps the Jigging Rap 4-5 feet above grouped-up bass to force committed fish to swim up; when they reach the bait and you stop it, it glides naturally downward and that's when you get bit.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal fall fish pull tighter to cover and deeper structure at 8-18ft. The shad migration pauses but doesn't stop. Focus on the deepest fish in a given creek arm — channel bends and main creek points.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If bites dry up, try shaking in place for 10-15 seconds before moving — patience pays with plastics

briefing.headline: Crank it — winter clear water calls for aggressive cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with clear water and pre frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: KVD 300 Deep runs 10-12ft — go-to for highland reservoirs and clear natural lakes in cold water. Fish off main lake points, bluff walls, bridge pilings, riprap. Parallel deeper edges. Twitch-twitch-pause with LONG pauses. Bright colors in shad kill conditions so fish see it from distance.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — winter clear conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with clear water and pre frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for pre frontal: aggressive, faster retrieves — fish are feeding actively before the front.

Wheeler: Keep paddle tail swimbaits near the bottom — fishing too far off bottom is the biggest mistake; use lighter line (12-lb fluoro) and heavier head to achieve deeper running depth and better action

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, flat, point. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: Wheeler: In late February/early March, bass group up in underwater ditches 30-40 feet deep; targets them with underspin or small swimbait on light fluorocarbon.

KVD: In clear deep lakes with shad kills, fish often suspend. The higher they are in the water column, the more active they are. Fish off main lake points, humps, drops, centers of big bays — not near the bank. Alabama rig with spinners (keeps rig...

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable winter conditions position bass at 15-35ft on main lake structure — bluffs, steep points, channel bends. Fish are lethargic and grouped tight. Vertical presentations directly on structure are essential.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If moving baits aren't getting bit, slow down to a crawling retrieve or switch to a jig

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — winter stained conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with stained water and stable conditions, reaction presentations shine.

KVD: Blade bait is my #1 bait for covering water in super cold water. Fish get locked on the bottom — short hop it on edges, drops, outside edges of flats and weed lines. Use just heavy enough to get to the zone. Great for largemouth, smallmouth, and spots.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Slow and natural — winter stained water is prime soft plastic territory.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with stained water and stable conditions, soft plastics presentations shine.

Wheeler: Peg the sinker on baits with a lot of action (craw-style); leave it unpegged on gliding-action baits like tubes so the weight slides away for better glide.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Crank it — winter stained water calls for finesse cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with stained water and post frontal conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: In extreme cold on grass lakes, upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the bait down. Downsize to quarter or 3/8 oz for ultra-shallow flats. Fish it like a jig — short hopping, not just cast and reel. The shimmy/quiver fall is what makes the Red Eye Shad special.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

briefing.depthStrategy: Post-frontal winter is the toughest bite of the year. Fish retreat to the deepest structure available (20-35ft+) and barely move. Target steep structure where fish can access deep water without traveling — bluffs, vertical channel walls, bridge pilings.
adjustIf: If skies cloud up and wind picks up, re-introduce moving baits — the reprieve from high pressure activates fish
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — winter stained conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with stained water and post frontal conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for post frontal: slow down significantly — extended pauses, subtle movements, finesse presentations.

Wheeler: Big fish spots are big giant flats in 22-29 ft with tiny isolated rockpiles — a boulder in the middle of a rocky flat, or a hard-bottom spot. Only good for 1-2 fish and time-consuming to find, but that's what separates you

briefing.depthStrategy: Pre-frontal winter bass stage at 12-25ft on main lake structure. Falling pressure before a front is often the best winter fishing window. Fish move slightly shallower and feed more aggressively than normal winter behavior.
adjustIf: If the front stalls and conditions stabilize, dial back aggression and focus on higher-percentage structure
If you find a cleaner pocket or tributary, switch to natural presentations — fish in cleaner water see more lures
If bites dry up, try shaking in place for 10-15 seconds before moving — patience pays with plastics

briefing.headline: Crank it — winter muddy water calls for steady cranking on key structure.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with muddy water and stable conditions, cranking presentations shine.

KVD: In extreme cold on grass lakes, upsize line to 17-20lb fluoro to slow the bait down. Downsize to quarter or 3/8 oz for ultra-shallow flats. Fish it like a jig — short hopping, not just cast and reel. The shimmy/quiver fall is what makes the Red Eye Shad special.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable winter conditions position bass at 15-35ft on main lake structure — bluffs, steep points, channel bends. Fish are lethargic and grouped tight. Vertical presentations directly on structure are essential.

briefing.headline: Go small, go slow — winter muddy conditions demand finesse presentations.
briefing.gameplan:
In winter with muddy water and stable conditions, finesse presentations shine.

Johnston: On deep Lake Ontario smallmouth at 33 feet, use a drop shot with a 3/8-ounce weight in all conditions. On calm days, cast it out and let it sit — don't move it. On rough, windy days, cast past the fish and drag it through them.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

briefing.headline: Pitch it tight — winter muddy water puts a jig in its best element.

In winter with muddy water and stable conditions, jigs presentations shine.

briefing.headline: Cover water — winter muddy conditions reward moving baits that trigger reaction strikes.

briefing.headline: Vertical and precise — winter muddy conditions call for reaction baits on deep structure.

In winter with muddy water and stable conditions, reaction presentations shine.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.
